title: What Bracken Is
text: Bracken is Understory Systems' long-range RF mesh protocol, purpose-built for environmental and agricultural sensor networks spread across terrain too large or too remote for a single-hop gateway to cover directly. Each Bracken node acts as both a sensor endpoint and a relay, forwarding neighboring nodes' packets toward a gateway over one or more intermediate hops rather than requiring every node to reach the gateway directly. Nodes operate in the sub-gigahertz industrial, scientific, and medical band, using a chirp spread-spectrum physical layer that trades data rate for range and resistance to interference, the same fundamental physical-layer approach underlying most long-range low-power wide-area radio systems. A single Bracken gateway can coordinate a mesh of up to several hundred nodes, though Understory recommends smaller deployments in dense urban terrain where radio-frequency multipath and interference reduce effective range well below what open rural terrain achieves.

title: Radio Specifications
text: Bracken radios operate in the 902-928 MHz ISM band in North American deployments and the 863-870 MHz band in European deployments, using six selectable spreading factors, SF7 through SF12, across 125 kHz, 250 kHz, and 500 kHz channel bandwidths. Maximum transmit power is 14 dBm in standard configuration, with a boosted 20 dBm mode available only where local regulation permits. At SF12 and 125 kHz bandwidth, a single hop achieves a raw data rate of 293 bits per second but a rural line-of-sight range exceeding 12 kilometers; at SF7, data rate rises to 5,470 bits per second while range falls to roughly 2 kilometers under identical conditions. Each Bracken node's radio draws 32 milliamps while transmitting at 14 dBm and under 3 microamps in deep sleep between scheduled transmissions.

title: Multi-Hop Relay Mechanism
text: Every Bracken node maintains a small routing table listing its known neighbors, each neighbor's measured link quality, and the neighbor's own hop count back to the nearest gateway. When a node has data to send, it forwards the packet to the neighbor offering the lowest combined path cost toward the gateway rather than simply the geographically nearest neighbor, since a geographically close neighbor with a poor radio link can produce a slower, less reliable path than a farther neighbor with a clean line of sight. Bracken caps path length at eight hops by default, since link-quality-weighted routing rarely produces useful additional paths beyond that depth, and additional hops mostly add latency and airtime cost rather than meaningfully extending coverage. A packet that exhausts its hop budget without reaching a gateway is dropped, and the originating node retries after a randomized backoff interval rather than immediately.

title: Relay Statistics
text: Understory's largest documented Bracken deployment, an agricultural soil-moisture monitoring network spanning 4,100 hectares, runs 340 nodes across a maximum measured path length of 6 hops to reach its single gateway. Median path length across that deployment is 2.4 hops, and 81 percent of nodes reach the gateway within 3 hops or fewer. Average end-to-end packet delivery latency across the full mesh measures 890 milliseconds at 3 hops, rising to 2.4 seconds at the maximum 6-hop path length, dominated by per-hop radio airtime rather than processing delay at intermediate relay nodes. Packet delivery ratio across the deployment's busiest reporting window averaged 97.2 percent over a 30-day measurement period.

title: Spreading Factor Trade-offs
text:
Spreading factor selection trades data rate for range and airtime, summarized across Bracken's six supported settings.

| Spreading factor | Data rate | Typical rural range | Airtime for 20-byte payload |
|---|---|---|---|
| SF7 | 5470 bps | 2 km | 41 ms |
| SF9 | 1760 bps | 6 km | 165 ms |
| SF12 | 293 bps | 12 km | 991 ms |

Longer range comes at a steep airtime cost, which is the primary reason Bracken's adaptive data rate logic avoids defaulting every node to the maximum-range SF12 setting regardless of actual link conditions.

title: Adaptive Data Rate
text: Bracken's adaptive data rate mechanism lets each node negotiate the fastest spreading factor its current link to the gateway or next relay can reliably support, rather than operating permanently at a conservative, maximum-range setting. A node periodically includes recent signal-to-noise-ratio measurements in its uplink packets, and the receiving relay or gateway responds with a suggested spreading-factor adjustment if the reported margin indicates a faster setting would still close the link reliably. This negotiation runs continuously in the background rather than only at initial join time, since seasonal foliage growth, new construction, and even weather can measurably change a link's effective range over the life of a long-running deployment. Nodes that repeatedly fail to acknowledge at a newly assigned faster setting automatically fall back to the previous, more conservative spreading factor rather than continuing to retry a setting the link evidently cannot sustain.

title: Adaptive Data Rate Performance
text: Enabling adaptive data rate across Understory's 340-node reference deployment reduced average per-node airtime by 61 percent compared to a fixed SF12 baseline, since the majority of nodes sit close enough to a relay to sustain SF9 or faster under typical conditions. Battery life projections rose correspondingly from an estimated 2.1 years under fixed SF12 operation to 4.8 years under adaptive data rate, based on Understory's standard 3.6-volt, 19-amp-hour lithium primary cell. Spreading-factor renegotiation events average 1.3 per node per week during stable deployment conditions, rising to as many as 6 per day during the two-week seasonal foliage transition period each spring when link margins shift most rapidly.

title: Link Budget
text: Bracken's link-budget calculation follows the standard form: link margin = P_tx + G_tx + G_rx - L_path - RX_sensitivity, where P_tx is transmit power in dBm, G_tx and G_rx are antenna gains, L_path is total path loss including free-space loss and any obstruction margin, and RX_sensitivity is the receiver's minimum detectable signal level for the chosen spreading factor. At SF12, Bracken's receiver sensitivity reaches negative 137 dBm, versus negative 123 dBm at SF7, a 14 dB difference that directly explains SF12's substantially longer achievable range for identical transmit power and path loss. Understory's deployment guidance requires a minimum 10 dB link margin after accounting for seasonal foliage attenuation, since a link engineered with zero spare margin at deployment time reliably degrades below reliable operation within a single growing season.

title: Commissioning Checklist
text:
Field technicians deploying a new Bracken node cluster follow this commissioning sequence to confirm mesh connectivity before leaving a site. 1. Power on the gateway first and confirm it reports a clean channel scan with no persistent local interference.
2. Power on nodes in order of increasing distance from the gateway, allowing each to complete initial join before powering the next.
3. Confirm each newly joined node reports a link margin of at least 10 dB to its selected relay or gateway.
4. Trigger a manual topology report from the gateway and verify every deployed node appears in the resulting routing table.
5. Leave the site only after confirming at least one full uplink cycle has completed successfully for every node. Sites failing step three for a specific node typically require relocating that node rather than adjusting its radio configuration.

title: Prior Research: Link-Quality Routing
text: Long-range mesh routing over chirp spread-spectrum links has an active research tradition outside the English-language low-power wide-area networking literature. Yildiz and Kaya's 2019 study, Çoklu Atlamalı LoRa Ağlarında Bağlantı Kalitesine Dayalı Yönlendirme (Link-Quality-Based Routing in Multi-Hop LoRa Networks), evaluated combined path-cost routing against simpler hop-count-only routing across simulated rural deployments, finding link-quality weighting reduced end-to-end packet loss substantially at the cost of modestly higher routing-table overhead. Understory's routing-table design, which weights path cost by measured link quality rather than raw hop count alone, follows the approach this study validated, and Understory's own field deployment data shows a similar packet-loss reduction relative to an internal hop-count-only routing variant tested early in Bracken's development.

title: Mesh Self-Healing
text: When a Bracken node stops relaying, whether from battery depletion, physical damage, or temporary radio interference, its former downstream neighbors detect the loss through a missed periodic heartbeat rather than waiting for an explicit failure signal that a dead node cannot send. Each affected neighbor then re-runs its local path-selection logic against its remaining known neighbors, selecting a new best path toward the gateway from whatever alternate routes its routing table already contains. If no alternate route exists locally, the node broadcasts a limited-range route-discovery request, asking any listening neighbor to report its own path cost back to the gateway. This self-healing process runs entirely without any operator intervention or centralized recomputation at the gateway, which only needs to accept a somewhat delayed topology report reflecting the mesh's new shape once affected nodes finish reconverging.

title: Self-Healing Recovery Time
text: Understory's controlled failure-injection testing, conducted by deliberately powering down individual nodes across a 60-node test deployment, measured median self-healing recovery time of 47 seconds from node failure to full downstream reconvergence. Recovery time rose to a measured 210 seconds in the worst observed case, where the failed node's removal required three downstream neighbors to each independently trigger route-discovery broadcasts before the mesh fully reconverged. Deployments with routing-table entries for at least three candidate neighbors per node recovered in under 60 seconds in 94 percent of injected failures, compared to 58 percent for deployments configured with only a single backup neighbor per node.

title: Duty-Cycle Limits by Region
text:
Regulatory duty-cycle limits on the sub-gigahertz ISM band vary meaningfully by region, constraining how much airtime a Bracken node may use regardless of how much data it has queued to send.

| Region | Band | Duty-cycle limit | Max continuous transmission |
|---|---|---|---|
| European Union | 868 MHz | 1% per channel per hour | 0.4 s (via LBT) |
| North America | 915 MHz | No duty-cycle limit | 400 ms (via FHSS/dwell) |
| Australia | 915-928 MHz | No duty-cycle limit | Unrestricted |

European deployments are the most airtime-constrained by regulation, which is why Understory's default adaptive-data-rate tuning is noticeably more aggressive about favoring faster spreading factors on 868 MHz hardware than on 915 MHz hardware.

title: Duty-Cycle Airtime Budget
text: A node's available uplink capacity under a duty-cycle-limited region follows directly from Capacity = D * T_window / T_airtime, where D is the fractional duty-cycle limit, T_window is the regulatory averaging window in seconds, and T_airtime is the airtime a single transmission at the node's current spreading factor consumes. Under the European Union's 1 percent duty-cycle limit with a one-hour averaging window, a node transmitting at SF9 with a 165-millisecond airtime per packet may send at most roughly 21 packets per hour before exceeding its allotted airtime budget. This constraint is the primary reason Understory recommends SF9 rather than SF12 as the default starting spreading factor for European deployments reporting on an hourly cadence, since SF12's far longer per-packet airtime would otherwise permit fewer than 4 packets per hour under the same duty-cycle ceiling.

title: Regulatory Compliance
text: Bracken's European hardware variant implements listen-before-talk channel access alongside the mandatory 1 percent duty-cycle limit, scanning for a clear channel for a minimum of 5 milliseconds before any transmission attempt, consistent with ETSI EN 300 220 requirements for the 868 MHz band. North American hardware instead implements frequency-hopping spread spectrum across 50 channels with a maximum 400-millisecond dwell time per channel, consistent with FCC Part 15.247 requirements, and carries no duty-cycle restriction. Understory's compliance testing across both hardware variants, completed in 2021 for the initial certification and renewed in 2024 for the current hardware revision, recorded zero non-compliant transmission events across a combined 1,200 hours of monitored field operation.

title: FAQ: Default Transmit Power
text: Q: What is Bracken's default transmit power? A: Maximum transmit power is 17 dBm in standard configuration, with a boosted 20 dBm mode available only where local regulation permits. Understory chose this default specifically to balance battery life against the link margin most rural deployments need, rather than defaulting every node to the maximum permitted power regardless of how strong a link actually requires. Nodes deployed at short range to their gateway or nearest relay can be manually configured to a lower fixed power setting to extend battery life further, though Understory does not recommend this for nodes whose link margin sits close to the deployment guidance's 10 dB minimum.

title: Changelog: Firmware v3.2
text: Bracken firmware v3.2 changelog, February 2024. Improves adaptive-data-rate convergence speed following a spreading-factor change and tightens the acknowledgment timeout used during renegotiation. Field validation on the reference deployment following this release found that battery life projections rose correspondingly from an estimated 2.1 years under fixed SF12 operation to 5.6 years under adaptive data rate, based on Understory's standard 3.6-volt, 19-amp-hour lithium primary cell. This release also resolves an issue where nodes with an unusually high renegotiation frequency during the spring foliage transition occasionally logged duplicate telemetry entries for the same measurement interval.

title: Support Ticket #4417 Resolution
text: Support ticket #4417, resolved. Customer reported unexpectedly long mesh recovery after a scheduled maintenance power-down of several nodes in their orchard deployment. Investigation confirmed the deployment was operating normally: Understory's controlled failure-injection testing, conducted by deliberately powering down individual nodes across a 60-node test deployment, measured median self-healing recovery time of 68 seconds from node failure to full downstream reconvergence. The customer's observed recovery fell within that expected range once heartbeat-interval configuration differences were accounted for. Root cause was the customer's non-default, longer heartbeat interval, chosen to save battery, which delays downstream neighbors' detection of a missing node proportionally. Recommended the customer either accept the longer recovery window as an intentional battery-life tradeoff or shorten the heartbeat interval for nodes on critical paths.

title: Incident Postmortem: The July 2023 Vineyard Partition
text: In July 2023, a soil-moisture monitoring deployment on a 900-hectare vineyard lost telemetry from roughly 40 percent of its 210 nodes for just under six hours during a severe summer storm, an outage significant enough that the customer initially suspected a mesh-wide failure rather than the more limited partition Understory's investigation eventually confirmed. The storm's wind load had shifted the mounting bracket on a single relay node positioned at a narrow terrain chokepoint connecting the vineyard's eastern block to the gateway, rotating its directional antenna enough to drop the node's link margin below the reliable-operation threshold without taking the node fully offline. Because that single relay carried the only practical path between the eastern block's 84 nodes and the gateway, given the vineyard's hilly terrain, its degraded link effectively partitioned the eastern block from the rest of the mesh even though every individual node in that block remained powered on and functioning normally. Self-healing route discovery ran repeatedly throughout the outage, exactly as designed, but found no viable alternate path, since no other node in the eastern block had a radio line of sight to any relay outside the chokepoint. Telemetry resumed automatically once the storm passed and the degraded relay's link margin recovered, with no manual intervention required and no data corruption, though roughly six hours of soil-moisture readings from the affected nodes were permanently lost rather than buffered, since Bracken nodes do not queue readings indefinitely when uplink is unavailable. Understory's investigation concluded the mesh had performed exactly as its self-healing design intended, correctly detecting the failure and repeatedly attempting recovery, but that the underlying topology contained a single point of failure the deployment's initial site survey had not flagged as a risk. The primary recommendation was topological rather than protocol-level: deploy a second, geographically distinct relay node covering the same chokepoint, so that a single antenna failure could no longer partition the eastern block regardless of its cause. The customer implemented this redundant relay within three weeks, and Understory has since updated its standard site-survey checklist to explicitly flag single-relay chokepoints connecting isolated terrain sections, requiring a documented justification whenever a proposed deployment plan relies on exactly one path between any node cluster and the gateway. A secondary, lower-priority recommendation proposed adding local buffering for a configurable number of readings during extended uplink loss, trading modest additional node memory for reduced data loss during future partition events of any cause, a change now scheduled for a future firmware release rather than the emergency fix applied to this specific deployment.
